title: Needle felted rabbit
subtitle: A compact sitting rabbit in grey or white merino with wire-reinforced ears
excerpt: Needle felt a sitting rabbit roughly 10 cm tall from merino roving, with a wool-wrapped wire armature inside the ears to keep them upright. The body is worked in grey or cream merino; surface detail for the nose, eyes, and inner ear is added in smaller quantities of pink and dark wool.
sourceNotes: Sitting-rabbit needle felt sculpture is a standard beginner project in guild and workshop instruction. Method draws on armature and basic-form technique common to the genre.

attrs.body: Stab straight in and out at 90 degrees over a foam pad. Stab toward the foam, not toward your hand.
attrs.title: Needle safety

content.text: The rabbit is built in three sections: a rounded oval body, a smaller sphere for the head, and two long oval ears. The ears are stiffened using
so they stand up rather than flopping. Assemble body, head, and ears separately, then join them. Surface colour for the nose, inner ear, and eyes is added last with small wisps of contrast colour.

items.name: Pink merino roving (inner ear and nose)
Thin florist wire or pipe-cleaner wire for ears

content.text: Pull about 15 g of grey roving into a rectangle and roll it into a tight cylinder. Wrap additional roving around the outside to build an oval body roughly 7 x 5 cm. Begin stabbing with the size-36 needle all over the surface, rotating the form constantly, until it is firm enough not to lose its shape when held. The body should feel dense throughout.

Roll about 6 g of roving into a ball roughly 4 cm in diameter. Needle-felt to a firm sphere. Shape the front of the face slightly flatter by stabbing that surface more densely. To create a slight muzzle bump, add a small pinch of roving to the front-centre and needle-felt it in place with the size-40 needle.

Cut two lengths of wire, each about 6 cm. Wrap each wire tightly in a thin layer of grey roving along its length, leaving 1 cm of bare wire at the base. Lay each wrapped wire on the foam mat and needle-felt the roving firmly onto itself until you have a narrow oval paddle shape, wider in the middle and pointed at the tip. Needle-felt a thin wisp of pink roving along the inner surface of each ear.

Hold the head against the top of the body and needle-felt them together by stabbing through the join from multiple angles. Insert the bare wire at the base of each ear into the top of the head at the correct position and needle-felt roving over the insertion point to secure. Add a small ball of white roving at the rear of the body for the tail and needle-felt into place.

Use the size-40 needle for all surface detail: a small pink oval for the nose, two tiny dark circles for the eyes. Felt each feature by placing a very small wisp of contrast roving on the surface and stabbing in a tight circle until locked in. Step back and check the balance of features before proceeding; adding more is easy, removing what has been felted in is not.

items.fix: Needle-felt the join by stabbing from several angles, pulling fibres from each section into the other. The join should be at least as firm as the body core.
items.problem: The head separates from the body when handled

items.fix: Add more roving to the base of the ear and needle-felt it to the head at a slightly outward angle so the ear leans away from the head naturally.
items.cause: Wire wrapped too loosely in roving or ears not felted firmly enough along their length
items.problem: The ears droop despite the wire

items.fix: Use the finest needle (size 40) and the smallest possible wisp of dark roving. Stab in a very tight circle to keep the boundary clean.
items.cause: Too much roving used for the spot, or too large a needle
items.problem: Eye spots bleed into the surrounding wool
